data.title: Gold Rebounds After Thursday's Fall, Remains Well Supported — Market Talk
data.description:
0741 GMT - Gold prices rise in early trade after settling lower in the previous session, weighed down by a stronger U.S. dollar and cautious optimism surrounding the Federal Reserve's policy outlook. "The Fed's tone on future rate cuts was less dovish than expected after its September meeting concluded," ANZ analysts say. Gold futures edge 0.2% higher to $3,685.20 a troy ounce, while the U.S. dollar index trades 0.1% higher at 97.45. Still, the precious metal remains up nearly 10% this month and is expected to continue benefiting from anticipated U.S. rate cuts and an uncertain geopolitical backdrop, as well as robust central-bank buying and ETFs inflows. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0727 GMT - Betting on steepening government bond yield curves is likely to be rewarding, although many investors favor such trades, Jefferies' Mohit Kumar says in a note. Eurozone bond yield curves steepened Thursday--implying wider spreads between short- and long-dated bond yields--following Germany's upward revision of debt issuance in the fourth quarter. "As we have argued before, we like steepeners, but the whole world likes steepeners," says the global economist. Jefferies had taken a step back from steepeners some days ago tactically--or in the short term--as the trade was getting consensus on Jefferies' positioning indices, Kumar says. "Our medium term view remains one of steepeners, but we would wait for a bit more cleanup in positions and better levels," he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0726 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds climb following the release of the public sector finances data. The U.K. public borrowing for August increased to 18 billion pounds, higher than the 13.5 billion-pound consensus forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. "This is going to lead to calls about the sustainability of public finances, and the need for tax rises at the upcoming Budget," XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. The 10-year gilt yields climbed 4 basis points earlier to a two-week high of 4.711% before retreating to last trade at 4.700%, Tradeweb data show. The 30-year gilt yields are at 2-week high of 5.549%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0721 GMT - U.K. public-sector borrowing data highlights the deteriorating nature of public finances even if the economy hasn't been terribly weak, Capital Economics' Paul Dales says in a note. Net borrowing came in at 18 billion pounds in August, well above the 12.5 billion pounds expected by the OBR budget watchdog. That is more than 11 billion pounds over the OBR's forecast in March, Dales notes. It suggests the U.K. treasury chief Rachel Reeves will have to raise 28 billion pounds mostly through higher taxes in the budget due in November if she wants to stick to her self-imposed guidelines, he writes. However, there are signs the economy isn't too weak, with separate data showing retail sales rising for a third straight month in August, Dales adds. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0712 GMT - The long-end driven selloff in bond markets is put to test as a tangible trigger for the 7-8 basis point increase in 30-year eurozone government bond yields on Thursday remains absent, says Commerzbank Research's Hauke Siemssen in a note. The German Finance Agency's funding increase for the fourth quarter "possibly served as an excuse to sell, while upbeat U.S. macro data in the afternoon caused further headwinds from U.S. Treasurys," the rates strategist says. Commerzbank Research sees chances for markets to stabilise, with buyers potentially emerging for German Bunds at yields above 2.70%. The 10-year Bund yield is up 2.8 basis points at 2.742%, while the 30-year Bund yield is up 2.8 basis points at 3.327%, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0708 GMT - Eurozone government bond yields rise, tracking yields of the U.S. peers. The French 10-year OAT yield rises somewhat less that the 10-year German Bund yield but it could remain prone to further increases as the new French Prime Minister aims to push next year's budget through the parliament. On Thursday, French bonds widened against German peers amid widespread protests in France against the government's spending cut plans. "This increases the pressure on new French PM Lecornu with Lecornu so far having given limited indications of concessions he will make in the 2026 budget to get it approved by parliament," says Danske Research's Jesper Fjarstedt in a note. The 10-year Bund yield is up 2.4 basis points at 2.738%, the 10-year OAT yield is up 1.8 basis points at 3.542%, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0659 GMT - Japan's Nikkei Stock Average closed 0.6% lower at 45045.81 after the Bank of Japan said it would start gradually selling its holdings of domestic equity exchange-traded funds and real estate investment trusts. The Tokyo Stock Exchange REIT Index dropped 0.3% to 1931.81. Retail and electronics stocks led the declines. Fast Retailing fell 4.5% and TDK dropped 3.3%. As the BOJ move showed it remains on its tightening path, the two-year Japanese government bond yield rose 2.5 bps to 0.905%, the 10-year yield gained 4.0 bps to 1.635%, while the 30-year yield fell 3.5 bps to 3.155%. USD/JPY was at 147.83, compared with 147.99 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0650 GMT - The dollar edges higher after stronger U.S. data eased fears about a weak economic outlook, particularly in the labor market, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Initial jobless claims fell 33,000 to 231,000 last week, below the 240,000 expected by economists in a WSJ survey. The Philadelphia Fed's manufacturing activity index jumped to 23.2 in September, above the 2.0 forecast. "All round, that helped to lift the mood on the U.S. outlook, particularly after several prints in early September had come in on the downside," the analysts say. The Federal Reserve resumed cutting interest rates Wednesday amid evidence of a cooling labor market. The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 97.482. It reached a three-and-a-half-year low of 96.218 Wednesday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0637 GMT - The active rating cycle for eurozone sovereigns is likely to persist with increasing convergence between semicore and peripheral countries, Citi Research's Aman Bansal says in a note. "Year-to-date changes in EMU-11 sovereign ratings have already surpassed annual totals since 2018," the rates strategist says. This gathered pace last week with France's downgrade to A+ stable by Fitch Ratings, alongside upgrades for Portugal (A stable) by Fitch and Spain (A+ stable) by S&P Global Ratings, he says. "For France, this was the first downgrade out of double-A territory and resulted in its lowest average rating since the formation of euro area, while the ratings for Spain and Portugal were taken to their highest since the euro crisis," Bansal says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0628 GMT - Sterling falls to a six-week low against the euro and a one-week low versus the dollar after data showed the U.K. government borrowed more than expected in August. Public sector net borrowing rose to 18 billion pounds, above the 13.5 billion pounds forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. That adds pressure on Treasury chief Rachel Reeves ahead of November's autumn budget where tax hikes are expected to plug a deficit. Separate data showed U.K. retail sales rose 0.5% month-on-month in August, as expected. It comes after the Bank of England left interest rates unchanged Thursday. Sterling falls to a low of $1.3497 after the data from $1.3543 beforehand. The euro rises to a high of 0.8718 pounds from 0.8688. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0628 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields rise in Asian trade, adding to an increase of global yields on Thursday. The U.S. data calendar is thin, while Mary Daly, President of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco delivers a speech at AI Implications for Workforce Development and Economic Mobility event. "The Fed's 'blackout' period is now at an end, meaning we'll receive not only remarks from 2027 voter Daly this evening, but should also at some stage get a statement from Gov. Miran explaining his dissent [at Wednesday's rate decision]," says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. The two-year Treasury yield rises 1 basis point to 3.576%; the 10-year Treasury yield rises 3.1 basis points to 4.134% and the 30-year Treasury yield rises 3.8 basis points to 4.757%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0546 GMT - The jury is still out on this tug of war between U.S. growth and inflation, says Federated Hermes' John Sidawi in a note. The impact of global tariffs has yet to be fully realised and the question of whether fiscal impulses will be sufficient to offset the drag of import taxes remains a large unknown, the senior portfolio manager for international fixed income says. "Much to the dismay of the FX and interest rate market the direction of US monetary policy remains very clear," but it was the journey that both markets mispriced Wednesday, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Asian currencies weaken on upbeat dollar; stocks falter
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indonesia's rupiah at 4-month low
  • MSCI EM currency gauge off a 10-week high
  • S.Korean won at 2-week low
  • Singapore stocks down for 6th day
  • Indonesia stocks hover above 8,000-level
  • Thai c.bank intervenes to slow baht appreciation

By Sameer Manekar

Emerging Asian currencies fell against a resurgent dollar on Friday while stocks retreated, as investors reassessed their positions after the U.S. Federal Reserve signalled a more calibrated approach to further monetary policy easing.

Stock markets in developing Asia were broadly on the back foot. The MSCI gauge of EM Asia equities (.MIMS00000PUS) fell 0.6% but held just below a four-year peak.

Singapore's FTSE Straits Times drifted lower for sixth consecutive session after reaching a record high on September 12, and stocks in Taiwan TWSE:TAIEX and South Korea slipped from record peaks scaled the previous day.

However, most equity benchmarks in the region were headed for weekly gains, largely because of the recent rally to record or multi-year highs in the run-up to the Fed's rate verdict.

A dovish Fed has given Asian central banks greater flexibility to ease monetary policy without triggering currency pressures, thereby supporting growth and further boosting investor risk appetite.

The MSCI EM Asia index (.MIMS00000PUS) advanced roughly 1% this week, while stocks in Indonesia and the Philippines were set to add 2% and 3%, respectively.

"The Fed signalling a rate cut cycle should continue to support risk appetite, so long as U.S. data does not materially deteriorate from here," wrote Nomura analysts led by equity strategist Chetan Seth.

"We think valuations of larger Asian markets are on the higher side, but not yet at extreme levels."

EM currencies were broadly lower against the dollar, with an MSCI index of global EM units (.MIEM00000CUS) slipping for a second consecutive day after reaching a 10-week high earlier in the week.

Malaysia's ringgit fell to 4.210 per dollar, its lowest in a week, and the rupiah sank to a four-month low of 16,585 a dollar. The latter has lost 1.7% since the reputed finance minister, Sri Mulyani Indrawati, was abruptly sacked on September 8, with Wednesday's surprise rate cut deepening the rupiah's decline.

The policy easing helped Indonesia stocks zoom past the 8,000-mark on Wednesday. The index was on course for its best week in five, even as concerns over fiscal discipline and Bank Indonesia's autonomy persisted.

South Korea's won slipped to a two-week low of 1,396.41 points, while Taiwan's dollar drifted lower from a one-month high.

Thailand's equities were largely steady after dropping 0.7% the previous day, while the baht hovered around 31.85 per U.S. dollar.

The Bank of Thailand intervened earlier in the day to slow the currency, which has risen more than 7% this year and is a threat to the export and tourism-reliant economy.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** Yield on Indonesia's 10-year government bond (ID10YT=RR) at 6.317%

** BOJ to unwind ETF holdings as board dissent signals hawkish shift; Nikkei slips from record high

** Indonesia FM says no need to change "arbitrary" fiscal rules

** Short bets on rupiah, rupee surge; bulls crowd yuan, baht - Reuters poll

data.title: India's GK Energy IPO fully subscribed on first day of bidding
data.description:
** GK Energy (GKEN.NS) IPO fully subscribed on day 1 of 3-day bidding process

** IPO subscribed 1x, as of 1.05 p.m. IST, with retail investors snapping up 1.5x shares on offer, exchange data shows

** Solar-powered pump systems maker is selling shares worth 4 billion rupees ($45.4 million) via fresh issue, existing shareholders selling 4.2 million shares

** Anchor investors got 1.39 billion rupees of shares on Sept 18

** GKEN sets IPO price band of 145-153 rupees per share

** Trading debut likely on Sept 26, according to co's red herring prospectus

($1 = 88.1637 Indian rupees)

data.title: Wickes's Strategy Give It a Competitive Edge in the Market — Market Talk
data.description: Home-improvement retailer Wickes Group's low pricing strategy positions it as one the most cost-effective options in the market, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Although profitability decline in recent years reflects a combination of headwinds such as postpandemic normalization and inflationary cost pressures, macro conditions have stabilized and operational leverage from investments is starting to take effect, Jefferies says. "We believe Wickes' value-led model, majority own-brand portfolio, and superior service provide a competitive edge in a fragmented U.K. home improvement market," Jefferies says. Jefferies starts coverage on the company with a buy rating on the stock and a 275 pence target price. Shares are up 4.4% at 212 pence, and are up 40% in the year-to-date. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0742 GMT - Home-improvement retailer Wickes Group's low pricing strategy positions it as one the most cost-effective options in the market, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Although profitability decline in recent years reflects a combination of headwinds such as postpandemic normalization and inflationary cost pressures, macro conditions have stabilized and operational leverage from investments is starting to take effect, Jefferies says. "We believe Wickes' value-led model, majority own-brand portfolio, and superior service provide a competitive edge in a fragmented U.K. home improvement market," Jefferies says. Jefferies starts coverage on the company with a buy rating on the stock and a 275 pence target price. Shares are up 4.4% at 212 pence, and are up 40% in the year-to-date. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0719 GMT - Medikaloka Hermina is poised to benefit from Astra International's doubling of its stake to 20%, Maybank Sekuritas Indonesia analysts say in a research report. This transaction supports the hospital-chain operator's long-term growth potential in Indonesia's private hospital sector and strengthens its alignment with a leading Indonesian conglomerate, the analysts say. Astra International's increased involvement is likely a mutually beneficial development. Synergies between the two companies and the success of Medikaloka Hermina's strategy to target the mid- to high-end market should serve as additional upside to its growth. The stock remains one of the brokerage's top picks in the sector, with a buy rating and a target price of IDR1,800.00. Shares are 0.6% lower at IDR1,675.00.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0712 GMT - Scout24's acquisition of Spain's Fotocasa and Habitaclia appears "judiciously opportunistic and level-headed," Jefferies analysts say in a note. The economics of the deal look solid, they say. The German company "has bucked the prevailing trends of European online classifieds operators avoiding M&A that involves either challengers or going crossborder," according to Jefferies. Shares trade 1.8% lower at 106.70 euros. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0659 GMT - Japan's Nikkei Stock Average closed 0.6% lower at 45045.81 after the Bank of Japan said it would start gradually selling its holdings of domestic equity exchange-traded funds and real estate investment trusts. The Tokyo Stock Exchange REIT Index dropped 0.3% to 1931.81. Retail and electronics stocks led the declines. Fast Retailing fell 4.5% and TDK dropped 3.3%. As the BOJ move showed it remains on its tightening path, the two-year Japanese government bond yield rose 2.5 bps to 0.905%, the 10-year yield gained 4.0 bps to 1.635%, while the 30-year yield fell 3.5 bps to 3.155%. USD/JPY was at 147.83, compared with 147.99 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0650 GMT - The dollar edges higher after stronger U.S. data eased fears about a weak economic outlook, particularly in the labor market, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Initial jobless claims fell 33,000 to 231,000 last week, below the 240,000 expected by economists in a WSJ survey. The Philadelphia Fed's manufacturing activity index jumped to 23.2 in September, above the 2.0 forecast. "All round, that helped to lift the mood on the U.S. outlook, particularly after several prints in early September had come in on the downside," the analysts say. The Federal Reserve resumed cutting interest rates Wednesday amid evidence of a cooling labor market. The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 97.482. It reached a three-and-a-half-year low of 96.218 Wednesday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0628 GMT - The Thai property sector's outlook appears positive, as earnings for the overall residential segment likely bottomed out in 1H, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Natchaphon Rodjanarowan writes in a research report. The sector is likely to be supported by drivers including new project launches in 2H. Interest-rate cuts are also expected to boost residential purchasing power, the analyst says. "These factors benefit developers with funds to accumulate land banks at current prices and capitalize on future land price appreciation once the residential market recovers," the analyst adds. Maybank maintains a neutral view on Thailand's property sector and pegs AP (Thailand) as its top pick. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0619 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening slightly lower with IG calling the OMXS30 down 0.2% at around 2630. Market expectations of Fed policy contributed to the S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, Dow Jones Industrial Average and Russell 2000 closing at all-time highs yesterday, SEB chief emerging market strategist Erik Meyersson writes. As expected, the Bank of Japan kept interest rates unchanged today, and an interest rate hike will have to wait until October, he adds. A two-day informal meeting of EU finance ministers and central bank governors begins in Copenhagen today. How the proceeds of Russia's frozen assets can be better used to support Ukraine and another package of sanctions is likely to be discussed, he says. OMXS30 closed at 2634.84, OMXN40 at 2347.83 and OBX at 1569.51. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0612 GMT - Electricity Generating's acquisition of 30% stake in Indonesia's Chandra Daya Investasi unlocks value, CGS International's Rasmiman Sermprasert says in a research report. This stake is expected to contribute at least 11% of the Thai company's total profit per year over 2026-2027, the analyst estimates. Hence, the brokerage lifts its 2026 and 2027 core net profit forecasts for Electricity Generating by 57% and 37%, respectively, reflecting the earnings accretion from the Indonesian company, which is now a publicly listed entity in Indonesia. The brokerage raises the stock's rating to add from hold and the target price to THB130.00 from THB110.00. Shares are 0.4% higher at THB116.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0606 GMT - ComfortDelGro is expected to deliver near-term mid-teens net profit growth, resilient cash flows and a 6.3% dividend yield in 2026, says RHB analyst Shekhar Jaiswal. The bank highlights improving UK public transport margins, capacity for new mergers and acquisitions, and stable point-to-point revenue from taxis and private hires in Singapore as share price drivers. The Singapore-based transport operator has also pre-qualified for the contract to operate the Copenhagen Metro. While the short-term earnings impact may be modest, a successful bid would mark its strategic entry into continental Europe, enhance its profile for future global tenders and diversify earnings opportunities beyond Singapore, the UK, and Australia, Jaiswal adds. RHB maintains its buy rating on ComfortDelGro with a S$1.75 target price. Shares were recently at S$1.49. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0535 GMT - China's biggest bottleneck in reducing reliance on Nvidia chips is its limited chip manufacturing capability due to U.S. restrictions, say Jefferies analysts in a research note. A flurry of new local AI chips announcements recently have boosted market confidence in China's chip self-reliance. However, China's most advanced contract chip maker, SMIC, still faces constraints on producing 7-nanometer chips without advanced equipment, they say. Huawei's plan last year to roll out Ascend 910D using 5-nanometer technology hasn't materialized due to poor yield, the analysts say. Should U.S. and China reach a trade deal, there could be some potential easing of U.S. export restrictions on chip-making equipment, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0525 GMT - Hotel Shilla's earnings are poised to recover long term on its exit from Incheon Airport's 'DF1 section' duty-free store license, Nomura's Siwoo Kim says, noting the company's announcement this week. The South Korean company concluded that losses over the remaining contract period would exceed the termination penalty, the analyst says in a research report. The luxury hotel operator is likely to fully suspend its airport DFS operations in 1Q 2026. Nomura lifts its 2026 operating margin forecast for Hotel Shilla to 6.6% from 2.9% due to the shut-down of the loss-making operations. The brokerage raises the stock's rating to buy from neutral and its target price to KRW72,000.00 from KRW49,000.00. Shares are 3.0% higher at KRW54,600.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0506 GMT - The Bank of Japan's planned sale of domestic equity exchange-traded funds may be insignificant to the market in terms of its pace of selling. The BOJ says it intends to sell ETFs worth about 620 billion yen in market value annually, which is the same pace it had been selling its domestic stock holdings until July this year. Despite the BOJ's stock sales and policy-rate increases, the Japanese stock market has maintained its rising trend in recent years to hit record highs. The central bank purchased domestic stocks from banks in the early 2000s and in the wake of the 2008 global financial crisis as part of extraordinary measures to support the financial system. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

data.title: U.K. Housebuilders' Sales Hurt by Low Consumer Confidence, Higher Mortgage Rates — Market Talk
data.description: U.K. housebuilders shouldn't expect a material sales rate pickup before the government's budget statement in November, RBC Capital Markets analysts say in a note. This is due to subdued consumer confidence and a pickup in mortgage rates in recent weeks, the analysts say. Sales rates for 3Q are up 3% year-on-year but activity has slowed through September, they say. Persimmon, a moderately bearish indicator, has been increasing the frequency of incentive offers for customers in recent months, while pricing in the sector has been mostly falling on a weekly basis, RBC says. Still, mortgage approvals are now similar to prepandemic levels and in line with those seen in 2013-19, RBC says. Crest Nicholson shares are down 1.5%, followed by Persimmon, down 0.7%, and M.J. Gleeson, down 0.5%. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: European Stocks Cautious on Friday
data.description:
The STOXX 50 and STOXX 600 hovered near the flatline on Friday as investors digested a week filled with monetary policy decisions.

The Bank of Japan left interest rates unchanged as expected but surprised markets by announcing plans to begin unwinding its massive exchange-traded fund holdings.

Traders also monitored a call between US President Trump and Chinese President Xi for signals on trade negotiations.

On the corporate side, SAP (-0.9%) and ASML Holding (-1%) slipped into the red, while Airbus (+1.7%) and Banco Santander (+0.6%) posted gains.

data.title: Ford recalls nearly 102,000 vehicles over risk of door trim detachment, NHTSA says
data.description:
Ford Motor is recalling 101,944 Taurus sedans in the United States due to a defect that could cause door trim to detach while driving, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration said on Friday.

The issue involves the B-pillar trim on the driver and front passenger doors, which may come loose during vehicle operation. Detached trim pieces could fall onto the road, causing a road hazard, NHTSA said in the notice.

The recall affects vehicles from model years 2016 through 2019, the U.S. auto safety regulator said, adding that Ford dealers will inspect the affected vehicles and repair or replace the trim as needed, free of charge.

data.title: Thales selects Diadrom for Trust My Tech
data.description:
Diadrom Systems AB, a leading software company in Autotech and Autodefense, is entering a collaboration with the French defense and security group Thales through the international innovation program Trust My Tech, facilitated by Kista Science City. The program aims to identify and accelerate technologies that strengthen security, reliability, and digital sovereignty in tomorrow’s society.

“We have already demonstrated globally that our expertise in Autotech delivers. The next step is to show how that same competence can create commercial solutions within Autodefense. Through Trust My Tech, we gain a unique platform to put our knowledge into practice and deliver solutions that extend lifecycles, enhance reliability, and make a tangible difference for both the automotive and defense industries,” says Viktor Eliasson, CEO of Diadrom Holding AB.

The collaboration is fully aligned with Diadrom’s long-term strategy to strengthen its position as a trusted technology partner in secure software development and advanced vehicle diagnostics. Through Trust My Tech, Diadrom gains access to Thales’ global network, world-leading expertise, and potential joint projects that can accelerate the company’s growth and innovation capacity.

The management of Diadrom assesses that this collaboration is of strategic importance for the company’s development.

data.title: Diadrom Holding Enters Collaboration With Thales
data.description:
Diadrom Holding AB :

data.title: Iron ore logs fourth straight weekly gain on improved steel demand
data.description:
By Lucas Liew

Iron ore futures advanced on Friday, ending the week higher, buoyed by strengthening steel demand and pre-holiday restocking in major consumer China.

The most-traded January iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE) rose 0.81% to 807.5 yuan ($113.54) a metric ton. The contract ended the week up 0.88%.

The benchmark September iron ore (SZZFU5) on the Singapore Exchange was 0.27% higher at $105.55 a ton as of 0716 GMT, though it is down 0.14% for the week.

Steel demand is strengthening with the onset of the peak season, and restocking ahead of the Chinese National Day holiday is providing continued support to the ferrous metals sector. If downstream demand recovers more strongly than expected from late September to October, steel prices could see further gains, said broker Galaxy Futures.

According to data from consultancy Mysteel, China’s stockpiles of major carbon steel products declined 0.3% during September 12–18 from the previous week to 4.18 million tons.

Average daily hot metal output, an indicator of iron ore demand, reached 2.41 million tons in the week to September 19, up 171,900 tons from a year earlier, while blast furnace capacity utilisation rose by 6.29 percentage points year-on-year to 90.35%, according to data from Chinese financial information site Hexun Futures.

Top producer China's crude iron ore production was up 8.8% year-on-year in August at 81.63 million tons, while crude steel output was down for a third month on slow demand, data from the National Bureau of Statistics showed.

Other steelmaking ingredients on the DCE gained ground, with coking coal NYMEX:ACT1! and coke (DCJcv1) up 1.36% and 0.75%, respectively.

Steel benchmarks on the Shanghai Futures Exchange were mostly up. Rebar gained 0.73%, wire rod (SWRcv1) climbed 0.09%, and hot-rolled coil increased 0.18%, while stainless steel dipped 0.39%.

($1 = 7.1118 Chinese yuan)

data.title: Asian currencies weaken on upbeat dollar; stocks falter
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indonesia's rupiah at 4-month low
  • MSCI EM currency gauge off a 10-week high
  • S.Korean won at 2-week low
  • Singapore stocks down for 6th day
  • Indonesia stocks hover above 8,000-level
  • Thai c.bank intervenes to slow baht appreciation

By Sameer Manekar

Emerging Asian currencies fell against a resurgent dollar on Friday while stocks retreated, as investors reassessed their positions after the U.S. Federal Reserve signalled a more calibrated approach to further monetary policy easing.

Stock markets in developing Asia were broadly on the back foot. The MSCI gauge of EM Asia equities (.MIMS00000PUS) fell 0.6% but held just below a four-year peak.

Singapore's FTSE Straits Times drifted lower for sixth consecutive session after reaching a record high on September 12, and stocks in Taiwan TWSE:TAIEX and South Korea slipped from record peaks scaled the previous day.

However, most equity benchmarks in the region were headed for weekly gains, largely because of the recent rally to record or multi-year highs in the run-up to the Fed's rate verdict.

A dovish Fed has given Asian central banks greater flexibility to ease monetary policy without triggering currency pressures, thereby supporting growth and further boosting investor risk appetite.

The MSCI EM Asia index (.MIMS00000PUS) advanced roughly 1% this week, while stocks in Indonesia and the Philippines were set to add 2% and 3%, respectively.

"The Fed signalling a rate cut cycle should continue to support risk appetite, so long as U.S. data does not materially deteriorate from here," wrote Nomura analysts led by equity strategist Chetan Seth.

"We think valuations of larger Asian markets are on the higher side, but not yet at extreme levels."

EM currencies were broadly lower against the dollar, with an MSCI index of global EM units (.MIEM00000CUS) slipping for a second consecutive day after reaching a 10-week high earlier in the week.

Malaysia's ringgit fell to 4.210 per dollar, its lowest in a week, and the rupiah sank to a four-month low of 16,585 a dollar. The latter has lost 1.7% since the reputed finance minister, Sri Mulyani Indrawati, was abruptly sacked on September 8, with Wednesday's surprise rate cut deepening the rupiah's decline.

The policy easing helped Indonesia stocks zoom past the 8,000-mark on Wednesday. The index was on course for its best week in five, even as concerns over fiscal discipline and Bank Indonesia's autonomy persisted.

South Korea's won slipped to a two-week low of 1,396.41 points, while Taiwan's dollar drifted lower from a one-month high.

Thailand's equities were largely steady after dropping 0.7% the previous day, while the baht hovered around 31.85 per U.S. dollar.

The Bank of Thailand intervened earlier in the day to slow the currency, which has risen more than 7% this year and is a threat to the export and tourism-reliant economy.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** Yield on Indonesia's 10-year government bond (ID10YT=RR) at 6.317%

** BOJ to unwind ETF holdings as board dissent signals hawkish shift; Nikkei slips from record high

** Indonesia FM says no need to change "arbitrary" fiscal rules

** Short bets on rupiah, rupee surge; bulls crowd yuan, baht - Reuters poll

data.title: South Korean shares log fourth weekly gain on Fed cut
data.description:
Key points:
  • KOSPI falls, foreigners net sellers
  • Korean won weakens against dollar
  • South Korea benchmark bond yield rises
  • For the midday report, please clicknL2N3V600C

Round-up of South Korean financial markets:

** South Korean shares fell on Friday but logged their fourth weekly gain on the back of a widely-expected Federal Reserve rate cut. The won weakened, while the benchmark bond yield rose.

** Benchmark KOSPI closed down 16.06 points, or 0.46%, at 3,445.24 as of 0632 GMT. For the week, it was up 1.46% and logged the fourth straight weekly gain, the longest weekly winning stretch since June.

** Lee Kyung-min, Daishin Securities analyst, said there has been some pressure building up for profit-taking as the index reached record highs this week.

** Among index heavyweights, chipmaker Samsung Electronics fell 0.99%, while peer SK Hynix was flat. Battery maker LG Energy Solution slid 1.41%.

** Hyundai Motor and sister automaker Kia Corp were down 2.06% and down 0.49%, respectively. Steelmaker POSCO Holdings shed 0.53%, while drugmaker Samsung BioLogics fell 0.49%.

** The Fed, goaded by the risk of rising unemployment, lowered rates on Wednesday for the first time since December 2024 and indicated that more cuts would follow.

** Of the total 930 traded issues, 242 advanced, while 640 declined.

** Foreigners were net sellers of shares worth 308 billion won.

** The won was quoted at 1,396.1 per dollar on the onshore settlement platform , 0.51% lower than its previous close.

** In offshore trading, the won was quoted at 1,395.8 per dollar, down 0.6% on the day, while in non-deliverable forward trading its one-month contract (KRW1MNDFOR=) was quoted at 1,393.2.

** The KOSPI has risen 43.58% so far this year.

** The won has strengthened 5.5% against the dollar so far this year.

** In money and debt markets, December futures on three-year treasury bonds (KTBc1) lost 0.11 point to 107.29.

** The most-liquid three-year Korean treasury bond yield (KR3YT=RR) rose by 2.7 basis points to 2.430%, while the benchmark 10-year yield rose by 6.2 basis points to 2.790%.

data.title: U.S. Futures Slip, European Stocks Mixed
data.description:
U.S. futures for the S&P 500 were down 0.1% and futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average declined 0.1%. Changes in futures do not necessarily predict movements after the opening bell.

In Europe, the Stoxx Europe 600 fell 0.1% in morning trading. Fresnillo rose 3.7% and Man Group climbed 3.7%. On the other hand, Kuehne + Nagel Intl dropped 5.6%, and Thule Group lost 3.7%. The FTSE 100 was flat. Other stocks in Europe were mixed as France's CAC 40 gained 0.4% and Germany's DAX held steady.

The Wall Street Journal Dollar Index rose 0.1% to 94.82.

In commodities, Brent crude was flat at $67.45 a barrel, and WTI crude fell 0.5% to $63.23 a barrel. The European benchmark price for natural gas, the Dutch futures contract TTF, was down 0.3% to 32.85 euros a megawatt hour.

The German 10-year Bund yield climbed by 2 basis points to 2.749% from 2.725%, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury rose by 2 basis points to 4.131% from 4.108%. Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions.

Stocks in Asia were down as Japan's Nikkei 225 index dropped 0.6%, and Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell 0.2%. China's benchmark Shanghai Composite declined 0.3%.

-This article was automatically generated by MarketWatch.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: New Projects Lift Angola's Crude Oil Output — Market Talk
data.description: Angola's crude oil production will likely rise 1.7% in 2026, boosted by new projects that have come on stream ahead of schedule in Africa's second-largest producer, says Oxford Economics Africa. The oil projects, run by France's TotalEnergies and Italy's Eni, helped boost production in August to 1.10 million barrels a day from 1.08 million barrels a day in July, and will continue to offset losses blamed on the country's ageing oil facilities. "We now anticipate that production will reach an estimated 1.11 million barrels a day early next year, bouncing back moderately to a full-year average of 1.14 million barrels a day in 2026," Oxford says. "Overall, the prospect for moderately higher oil output over the medium term is positive." (nicholas.bariyo@wsj.com;@Nicholasbariyo)

data.title: ExxonMobil has postponed two chemical waste treatment projects in Europe
data.description:
ExxonMobil, opens new tab is pausing 100 million euros ($118.4 million) of investment in European plastic recycling because of draft EU rules that determine the recycled content in a final product. as per Reuters.

The U.S. energy producer has two projects for chemical recycling at existing plants in Rotterdam and Antwerp to process 80,000 metric tons of plastic waste per year.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.However, Senior Vice President Jack Williams told Reuters in an interview the two projects had now been paused because of the draft EU rules he said discriminated against using existing petrochemicals sites versus standalone facilities.

"Everything else is on track. We've had local support," he said. "We want to make these investments... The only thing standing between us and doing this project is EU policy."At issue is a draft law to calculate the recycled content based on the mass of waste going into the system and the mass of the output.

ExxonMobil says it significantly favours standalone technologies where the path from plastic waste to output is clearer and penalises more complex integrated facilities into which fossil feedstocks are fed.

Williams said that, based on the draft law, its facilities would get less than half of the credits due.

The draft has been the subject of a public consultation that ended a month ago. Industry groups and companies, including Finland's Neste, share Exxon's view.

The EU has targets to increase plastic recycling rates, such as 30% recycled content in plastic bottles by 2030.The industry says it needs to combine mechanical recycling that reprocesses waste without changing its chemical structure and chemical recycling, which can process more complex plastics by breaking them down into basic chemicals.

Williams also said that although U.S. import tariffs were not a significant problem for the company, EU regulation was.

He specifically urged the EU to repeal the Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) that requires larger companies to check if their supply chains use forced labour or cause environmental damage. Williams said it was complex, bureaucratic, costly, in some cases unachievable and applied beyond the EU. The EU has already eased the rules and delayed its implementation.

mrchub.com

data.title: UK 10-Year Gilt Yield Rises as UK Budget Deficit Weigh on Sentiment
data.description:
UK 10-year gilt yields climbed to 4.7%, a two-week high, after public finances data showed a sharply wider deficit.

Public sector net borrowing hit £18 billion in August, well above forecasts of £12.8 billion and the highest for the month in five years.

Borrowing for the first five months of the fiscal year totaled £83.8 billion, £11.4 billion above OBR projections, with earlier months revised up by £5.9 billion.

The figures highlight fiscal strains ahead of November’s budget, when Chancellor Reeves is expected to announce tax rises.

Rising borrowing costs come as bond markets remain jittery globally over debt levels.

Meanwhile, the BoE held rates at 4% in a 7–2 vote, slowed QT to £70 billion, and reaffirmed a cautious easing stance.

In the US, the Fed cut rates 25 bps and signaled more to come, though Powell framed the move as risk management.
